title: Just the Job
summary: A night-shift hospital cleaner in Toronto and a patient's daughter disagree about whether the quiet companionship offered to the dying counts as care or merely the work.

body.speaker: Priya
body.text: Please don't make it into something it isn't. I mop the ward, I empty the bins, and on the nights your father couldn't sleep I sat for a few minutes because the room was already clean. There was no plan to it, no kindness I had decided on in advance. What you're calling devotion was really just the gaps in my shift, and gaps are not the same as gifts.

body.speaker: Elena
body.text: I'm not trying to flatter you. I'm trying to understand why he remembered your name and forgot almost everyone else's. By the last months he could not always place his own grandchildren, yet he asked for you by name. The nurses rotated, the doctors changed, but you were the constant. Surely that meant something, whatever the job description says.

body.speaker: Priya
body.text: Memory is strange near the end. He may have held on to my name because nothing was expected of it. A nurse arrives wanting something — your arm, your answer, your consent. I arrived wanting nothing. Perhaps that is why I was easy to keep.

body.speaker: Elena
body.text: That's exactly my point, though you keep dressing it as modesty. You arrived wanting nothing — which is the rarest thing a frightened person can be offered. When you are dying, everyone around you seems to be measuring you, hurrying you, needing you to be a little better than you are. You asked nothing of him, and so he could simply be. You may not have a title for it, but he felt it, and I think you did too.

body.speaker: Priya
body.text: I won't deny he was good company. We talked about cricket, about the snow he never got used to after forty years here. But I worry when families turn a cleaner into an angel. It lets the system off the hook. If kindness is a private gift I gave, then no one has to pay for it or build it into the rota.

body.speaker: Elena
body.text: Now that is a fair charge, and it stings. I do want to believe a stranger loved my father, because it's easier than admitting the ward was understaffed and the loving fell to whoever had a spare ten minutes. So perhaps I should be angrier at the hospital.

body.speaker: Priya
body.text: Don't go that far either. Be angry at the rota and grateful to me; the two aren't in competition. What I resist is only the story where my decency excuses their arithmetic. Pay people to clean, and some will also, quietly, keep the dying company. But you cannot put that in a budget line, nor demand it.

body.speaker: Elena
body.text: Then let me say the one thing you can't argue away. Whatever it was — the gaps, the wanting nothing — it reached him when his own children couldn't always be there. We had jobs, and flights, and our own children to put to bed. You were there at three in the morning when none of us could be. I don't need you to call it care. I only need you to let me.

annotations.phrase: make it into something it isn't
annotations.meaningVi: biến nó thành thứ mà nó không phải là
đổ thùng rác
những khoảng trống trong ca làm việc của tôi
sự tận tâm, lòng tận tuỵ
tâng bốc, nịnh bạn
các y tá thay phiên nhau
yếu tố không đổi, người luôn ở đó
dù bản mô tả công việc nói gì đi nữa
giữ lấy/nhớ tên tôi
không đòi hỏi gì cả
dễ giữ lại (trong trí nhớ)
nguỵ trang nó thành sự khiêm tốn
điều hiếm hoi nhất
người bạn đồng hành dễ chịu

annotations.phrase: turn a cleaner into an angel
annotations.meaningVi: biến một người lao công thành thiên thần

annotations.phrase: lets the system off the hook
annotations.meaningVi: khiến hệ thống thoát trách nhiệm
đưa nó vào lịch phân ca
một lời buộc tội xác đáng
nó nhói đau, làm tổn thương
thiếu nhân lực
việc yêu thương rơi vào tay
sự tử tế của tôi biện minh cho phép tính (cắt giảm) của họ
một khoản mục ngân sách
ở thế cạnh tranh, đối nghịch nhau
tranh cãi để phủ nhận, gạt bỏ
chạm tới được ông ấy (về mặt tình cảm)
hãy để tôi (gọi nó như vậy)

grammarNotes.title: Cleft sentence: What you're calling X was really Y
grammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What you're calling devotion was really just the gaps in my shift' — câu chẻ dùng để định nghĩa lại điều đối phương vừa nói.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What you're calling devotion was really just the gaps in my shift' is a cleft that redefines the other speaker's claim.

grammarNotes.title: Concession + contrast: whatever / though
grammarNotes.bodyVi: 'whatever the job description says' và 'though you keep dressing it as modesty' là các mệnh đề nhượng bộ, thừa nhận một phần rồi phản biện.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'whatever the job description says' and 'though you keep dressing it as modesty' are concessive clauses that grant a point before pushing back.

grammarNotes.title: Nominalisation for abstract argument
grammarNotes.bodyVi: 'my decency excuses their arithmetic' biến hành động (tử tế, tính toán) thành danh từ trừu tượng để lập luận cô đọng.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'my decency excuses their arithmetic' uses nominalisation to compress an argument into abstract nouns.

translationVi:
Priya: Xin đừng biến nó thành thứ mà nó không phải là. Tôi lau sàn khu bệnh, tôi đổ thùng rác, và vào những đêm cha cô không ngủ được tôi ngồi lại vài phút vì căn phòng đã sạch sẵn rồi. Chẳng có toan tính gì cả, chẳng có lòng tốt nào tôi định trước. Cái mà cô gọi là sự tận tâm thực ra chỉ là những khoảng trống trong ca làm của tôi, và khoảng trống thì không phải là món quà.
Elena: Tôi không cố tâng bốc cô. Tôi đang cố hiểu vì sao ông ấy nhớ tên cô mà quên gần như tên mọi người khác. Đến những tháng cuối, ông không phải lúc nào cũng nhận ra cháu mình, vậy mà ông lại gọi tên cô. Y tá thay phiên, bác sĩ đổi người, nhưng cô là yếu tố không đổi. Chắc chắn điều đó phải có ý nghĩa gì đó, dù bản mô tả công việc nói gì đi nữa.
Priya: Trí nhớ kỳ lạ lắm khi gần đến cuối. Có lẽ ông ấy giữ lấy tên tôi vì không ai mong đợi điều gì ở nó. Một y tá đến là muốn điều gì đó — cánh tay ta, câu trả lời của ta, sự đồng ý của ta. Tôi đến mà chẳng đòi hỏi gì. Có lẽ vì thế mà tôi dễ được giữ lại trong trí nhớ.
Elena: Đó chính xác là điều tôi muốn nói, dù cô cứ nguỵ trang nó thành sự khiêm tốn. Cô đến mà chẳng đòi hỏi gì — đó là điều hiếm hoi nhất mà một người đang sợ hãi có thể được trao. Khi ta đang hấp hối, mọi người quanh ta dường như đang đong đếm ta, hối thúc ta, cần ta phải khá hơn con người thật của ta một chút. Cô chẳng đòi hỏi gì ở ông, nên ông có thể cứ là chính mình. Cô có thể không có tên gọi cho nó, nhưng ông ấy cảm nhận được, và tôi nghĩ cô cũng vậy.
Priya: Tôi sẽ không phủ nhận ông ấy là người bạn đồng hành dễ chịu. Chúng tôi nói chuyện về cricket, về tuyết mà ông ấy chẳng bao giờ quen nổi sau bốn mươi năm ở đây. Nhưng tôi lo khi các gia đình biến một người lao công thành thiên thần. Nó khiến hệ thống thoát trách nhiệm. Nếu lòng tốt là một món quà riêng tư mà tôi trao đi, thì chẳng ai phải trả tiền cho nó hay đưa nó vào lịch phân ca.
Elena: Bây giờ thì đó là một lời buộc tội xác đáng, và nó làm nhói đau. Tôi đúng là muốn tin rằng một người lạ đã yêu thương cha tôi, vì điều đó dễ hơn là thừa nhận khu bệnh thiếu nhân lực và việc yêu thương rơi vào tay bất kỳ ai có dư mười phút. Vậy nên có lẽ tôi nên giận bệnh viện hơn.
Priya: Đừng đi xa đến thế. Hãy giận cái lịch phân ca và biết ơn tôi; hai điều đó không đối nghịch nhau. Điều tôi phản đối chỉ là cái câu chuyện trong đó sự tử tế của tôi biện minh cho phép tính cắt giảm của họ. Hãy trả tiền cho người ta để lau dọn, và một số sẽ, lặng lẽ, làm bạn với người sắp ra đi. Nhưng cô không thể đưa điều đó vào một khoản mục ngân sách, cũng chẳng thể đòi hỏi nó.
Elena: Vậy thì hãy để tôi nói một điều mà cô không thể tranh cãi để gạt bỏ. Dù nó là gì đi nữa — những khoảng trống, việc chẳng đòi hỏi gì — nó đã chạm tới được ông ấy khi chính con cái ông không phải lúc nào cũng có mặt. Chúng tôi có công việc, có những chuyến bay, và có con cái của mình phải dỗ ngủ. Cô đã ở đó lúc ba giờ sáng khi không ai trong chúng tôi có thể. Tôi không cần cô gọi nó là sự chăm sóc. Tôi chỉ cần cô để cho tôi gọi nó như vậy.

questions.prompt: What is Priya's central claim about what she did for Elena's father?
options: That it was professional nursing care
That it was simply what fitted into the empty moments of her cleaning shift
That she was specially assigned to him
That she was paid extra to keep him company
questions.explanation: Priya says 'What you're calling devotion was really just the gaps in my shift.'

questions.prompt: Why does Priya think the father held on to her name?
options: Because she reminded him constantly of it
Because she was the only Canadian-born staff member
Because, unlike the nurses, she wanted nothing from him
Because she was the youngest member of staff
questions.explanation: She contrasts the nurse who 'arrives wanting something' with herself: 'I arrived wanting nothing.'
questions.hint: See Priya's second turn, where she compares herself to the nurses.

questions.prompt: What danger does Priya see in families turning a cleaner into 'an angel'?
options: It excuses an understaffed system from having to pay for or plan such care
It encourages cleaners to neglect their real duties
questions.explanation: She warns it 'lets the system off the hook' so 'no one has to pay for it or build it into the rota'.
questions.hint: Look at Priya's turn about the 'private gift'.

questions.prompt: How does Elena respond to Priya's 'off the hook' argument?
options: She concedes it is fair and admits she would rather be grateful than angry at the hospital
She claims the hospital was well staffed
questions.explanation: Elena calls it 'a fair charge', admits 'it stings', and says she should be 'angrier at the hospital'.
questions.hint: See Elena's turn beginning 'Now that is a fair charge.'

questions.prompt: What does Priya mean by 'my decency excuses their arithmetic'?
options: Her kindness proves the budget is generous
Her individual kindness shouldn't justify the institution's cost-cutting
The hospital rewards decent staff financially
questions.explanation: She resists 'the story where my decency excuses their arithmetic' — her goodness should not cover for cuts.

questions.prompt: Which statement does Priya explicitly NOT make?
options: That the father was good company
That kindness cannot be demanded or budgeted
That Elena should be grateful and angry at once
That the hospital deliberately hired her to befriend patients
questions.explanation: Priya insists her companionship came from the 'gaps' in her shift, never that she was hired to befriend patients.
questions.hint: Look for a claim none of Priya's turns actually contains.

questions.prompt: What does the snow detail ('the snow he never got used to after forty years here') reveal about the father?
options: He had recently arrived in Canada
He was an immigrant who had lived in Toronto for decades
He worked outdoors all his life
questions.explanation: Forty years 'here' with snow he never adjusted to marks him as a long-settled immigrant.
questions.hint: See Priya's turn about cricket and snow.

questions.prompt: What is Elena's final request of Priya?
options: To accept payment for her kindness
Not to insist on calling it 'care', but to let Elena call it that
To visit the family after the funeral
questions.explanation: Elena says 'I don't need you to call it care. I only need you to let me.'

questions.prompt: How would you best describe the relationship between the two speakers' positions?
options: They flatly contradict each other and never agree
Each acknowledges a truth in the other's view while holding a different emphasis
Elena dominates and Priya gives in completely
questions.explanation: Priya concedes Elena's gratitude is legitimate; Elena concedes the system is at fault — each grants something true.
questions.hint: Notice where each speaker says the other is right.

questions.prompt: What does Priya mean when she says the two — anger and gratitude — 'aren't in competition'?
options: Elena can both blame the hospital and thank Priya at the same time
Priya wants Elena to choose one feeling
The hospital and Priya are rivals
questions.explanation: Priya tells Elena to 'Be angry at the rota and grateful to me; the two aren't in competition.'
questions.hint: See Priya's turn beginning 'Don't go that far either.'

questions.prompt: Why might Priya describe wanting nothing as 'the rarest thing a frightened person can be offered'?
options: Because most interactions near the end make demands, so undemanding company is unusually precious
Because patients prefer to be left alone
questions.explanation: It is Elena who reframes Priya's point this way, contrasting the demands of others with Priya's making none.
questions.hint: Elena turns Priya's modesty into this praise.

questions.prompt: What does Elena admit is 'easier' to believe?
options: That a stranger loved her father, rather than that the ward was understaffed
questions.explanation: She says believing 'a stranger loved my father' is 'easier than admitting the ward was understaffed'.
questions.hint: See Elena's turn responding to the 'off the hook' point.

questions.prompt: Which word best captures Priya's tone throughout?
questions.explanation: She repeatedly refuses to be turned into an 'angel' or have her work romanticised.
questions.hint: Notice what she keeps pushing back against.

questions.prompt: What can be inferred about why the nurses are described as 'rotating'?
options: Staff changed often, so no nurse was a steady presence for the father
They moved between hospitals each week
questions.explanation: Elena contrasts rotating nurses and changing doctors with Priya as 'the constant'.

questions.prompt: What broader point does the dialogue raise about care work?
options: That cleaners should be promoted to nurses
That informal kindness from low-status workers fills gaps an underfunded system leaves, but should not excuse that underfunding
That families should never thank staff
That hospitals are always well run
questions.explanation: Priya values the kindness yet warns against letting it 'excuse their arithmetic'.
questions.hint: Combine Priya's pride in the company she gave with her warning about the budget.

questions.prompt: Why does Elena say 'it stings' when she calls Priya's argument 'a fair charge'?
options: Because the truth that the loving 'fell to whoever had a spare ten minutes' is painful to accept
questions.explanation: Accepting that the system, not chosen devotion, shaped the care is painful for a grieving daughter.
questions.hint: Read what Elena admits right after 'it stings'.

cloze.template:
Priya: Please don't make it into something it isn't. I mop the ward, I empty the bins, and on the nights your father couldn't sleep I sat for a few minutes because the room was already clean. There was no plan to it, no kindness I had decided on in advance. What you're calling {{b1}} was really just the {{b2}} in my shift, and gaps are not the same as gifts.
Elena: I'm not trying to {{b3}} you. I'm trying to understand why he remembered your name and forgot almost everyone else's. By the last months he could not always place his own grandchildren, yet he asked for you by name. The nurses {{b4}}, the doctors changed, but you were the {{b5}}. Surely that meant something, whatever the job description says.
Priya: Memory is strange near the end. He may have held on to my name because nothing was expected of it. A nurse arrives wanting something — your arm, your answer, your {{b6}}. I arrived wanting {{b7}}. Perhaps that is why I was easy to {{b8}}.
Elena: That's exactly my point, though you keep dressing it as modesty. You arrived wanting nothing — which is the {{b9}} thing a frightened person can be offered. When you are dying, everyone around you seems to be measuring you, hurrying you, needing you to be a little better than you are. You asked nothing of him, and so he could simply be. You may not have a title for it, but he {{b10}} it, and I think you did too.
Priya: I won't deny he was good company. We talked about cricket, about the snow he never got used to after forty years here. But I worry when families turn a cleaner into an {{b11}}. It lets the system off the {{b12}}. If kindness is a private gift I gave, then no one has to pay for it or build it into the {{b13}}.
Elena: Now that is a fair charge, and it stings. I do want to believe a stranger loved my father, because it's easier than admitting the ward was {{b14}} and the loving fell to whoever had a spare ten minutes. So perhaps I should be angrier at the hospital.
Priya: Don't go that far either. Be angry at the rota and grateful to me; the two aren't in competition. What I resist is only the story where my {{b15}} excuses their arithmetic. Pay people to clean, and some will also, quietly, keep the dying company. But you cannot put that in a budget line, nor demand it.
Elena: Then {{b16}} me say the one thing you can't argue away. Whatever it was — the gaps, the wanting nothing — it reached him when his own children couldn't always be there. We had jobs, and flights, and our own children to put to bed. You were there at three in the morning when none of us could be. I don't need you to call it care. I only need you to let me.

blanks.explanation: Priya redefines what Elena calls 'devotion' as merely shift gaps.
'just the gaps in my shift' — the empty moments between tasks.
Priya was 'the constant' amid changing staff.
'your consent' completes Priya's list of what a nurse needs.
'I arrived wanting nothing' is the contrast she draws.
'easy to keep' — easy to hold on to in memory.
Elena calls it 'the rarest thing' a frightened person can receive.
'but he felt it' — even without a title.
Priya warns against turning a cleaner 'into an angel'.
'lets the system off the hook' — frees it from responsibility.
'build it into the rota' — the staff schedule.
'where my decency excuses their arithmetic' — her kindness covering for cuts.
'I only need you to let me' — let Elena call it care.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Priya insists that her kindness should not 'excuse their arithmetic' — that valuing one worker's compassion must not let an underfunded institution avoid responsibility. When informal generosity quietly fills the gaps a system leaves, does praising the individual help or harm the case for fixing the system? Explain your reasoning.
